lesson.menu_title: The Hard Conversation -- Giving Difficult Feedback
lesson.menu_desc: Give hard feedback in four moves: behavior, impact, question, ask -- 30 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 12 -- The Hard Conversation -- Giving Difficult Feedback
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Diogo Leal | Aula 12 -- The Hard Conversation

plan_row.tema: The Hard Conversation -- Giving Difficult Feedback
plan_row.foco: Behavior + impact (When... the impact is...) + I need you to + verb
plan_row.atividade: Feedback note + 1:1 role-play with a developer who gets defensive
plan_row.homework: Record a 2-min feedback: behavior, impact, question, ask

paras: Most managers give feedback badly, and they give it late. They wait for the review, they write a long speech, and then they say something vague: 'You need to be more proactive.' The person hears nothing useful, and nothing changes.

Good feedback is short, specific and about behavior. Not 'you are careless', but 'the last three sprints closed two days late, and the test team lost two days each time'. One is an attack on a person. The other is a fact, and a fact can be discussed.

Then comes the part most people forget: the question. 'What is getting in the way?' Ask it, and be quiet. The answer is usually the real problem -- and it is often something you can fix. Feedback is not a speech. It is a conversation, and the other person has to speak.

choices: Feedback should be saved for the yearly review, when you have more examples.
Good feedback is short, specific, about behavior -- and it ends with a question.
The manager should do most of the talking, to be clear.

items: 'You need to be more proactive' is specific feedback.
It is vague. Specific means a date, a number, one event.
Feedback should describe behavior, not the person.
'The last three sprints closed late' -- a fact, not an attack.
After the feedback, you should ask a question and listen.
'What is getting in the way?' -- then be quiet.

bank.label: The five lines of hard feedback
items: Can I give you some feedback? It is not easy, but it matters.
The last three sprints closed two days late.
The impact is that the test team loses two days, every sprint.
What is getting in the way?
I need you to flag the risk on day three, not on the last day.
You are strong technically, and I want this to be the only thing we fix.

items.situation: Open the conversation. She does not know why she is here.
tips: Say it is not easy. Do not smile it away.

items.situation: Describe the behavior in one sentence, with a number.
tips: A date, a number, one event.

items.situation: She says: 'It is not my fault.' Answer.
tips: Return to the fact, and to the impact.

items.situation: Name the impact on the team, not on you.
tips: Who pays for it? Say their name.

items.situation: Ask the question -- and then be quiet.
Close with one clear ask, and one true piece of praise.

pressure.title: Answer live
items: Are you saying I am a bad developer?
Why are you telling me this now, and not three sprints ago?
Does Sophia know about this conversation?

pressure.text: Answer first, fact second, ask third. Never negotiate the fact -- and never repeat the label she puts on herself.

listenings.text: Diogo, I read your sprint report. Three sprints late, and the reason is always the same person. You have not spoken to her, have you? I know it is uncomfortable. But if you do not say it, two things happen. She never gets better, because nobody told her. And the team sees that late is acceptable. Do not be harsh, and do not make a speech. Be specific: what happened, how many days, what it costs the test team. Then ask her one question, and be quiet. Ninety percent of the time the answer surprises you. And Diogo, do not send an email. Say it to her face.

Marina, thank you for the time. Can I give you some feedback? It is not easy, and I want to be specific. The last three sprints closed two days late. I am talking about the deadline, not about the quality of your code -- the migration script was the best work on the team. But when a sprint closes late, the impact is real: the test team loses two days, every time, and they work the weekend. So my question is simple. What is getting in the way? Please be honest with me. I am not here to blame you. I am here because I need you to flag the risk on day three, not on the last day, so we can adjust the plan together. If something is blocking you, tell me and I will remove it.

extra_audio.text: Diogo opens the conversation and asks if he can give feedback. He describes the behavior: the last three sprints closed two days late. Marina gets defensive, and says the tickets came late. Diogo names the impact on the test team, and asks what is getting in the way. Marina says she waits three days for a code review, and Diogo asks her to flag it on day three.
